title: Public initialize() function enables ownership theft on new proxy deployments via front-running
claim: The initialize() function in MarketCapSqrtController is public with no access control modifier (no onlyOwner, no initializer guard). When a new proxy is deployed via DelegateCallProxyManager.deployProxyOneToOne(), the implementation address is set but initialize() is never called by the deployer. An attacker monitoring the mempool can front-run the owner's initialization transaction to call initialize() and become the owner, seizing full control of the controller contract including the ability to deploy pools, set fees, and configure all protocol parameters.
impact: An attacker who front-runs initialization gains irrevocable ownership of the controller proxy. This grants complete control over pool creation, fee settings, exit fee recipients, token categories, and all privileged operations. All funds flowing through pools deployed by the compromised controller would be at risk. The attack requires only that a new proxy is deployed and the attacker sends a transaction before the legitimate owner.
paths: 1. Owner calls deployProxyOneToOne() on DelegateCallProxyManager to deploy a new controller proxy
2. The proxy is deployed and implementation is set, but initialize() is NOT called by the deployer
3. Attacker sees the deployment transaction in the mempool
4. Attacker sends initialize() call to the newly deployed proxy address with higher gas price
5. initialize() executes: defaultSellerPremium = 2; then _initializeOwnership() sets _owner = attacker
6. Attacker is now the permanent owner of the controller, with full control over all onlyOwner functions
7. Attacker can call prepareIndexPool(), setSwapFee(), setController(), setDefaultExitFeeRecipient(), etc.

title: updateMinimumBalance lacks onlyOwner modifier allowing anyone to manipulate token minimum balances
claim: The updateMinimumBalance() function is protected only by the _havePool modifier (which merely checks that the pool exists) but has NO onlyOwner access control. Every other pool management function (setSwapFee, setExitFeeRecipient, setController, setDefaultSellerPremium, updateSellerPremium) is properly gated with onlyOwner. This inconsistency allows any external caller to update the minimum balance of any unready token in any initialized pool. The function computes minimumBalance as poolValue * tokenPrice / 100 using _estimatePoolValue which reads raw ERC20 balances that can be inflated via direct token donations.
impact: An attacker can set minimum balances to extreme values: (a) by donating tokens to inflate _estimatePoolValue, then calling updateMinimumBalance to set an impossibly high minimum balance, permanently preventing the token from becoming 'ready' and bricking pool trading; or (b) by withdrawing tokens to deflate the pool value, setting an extremely low minimum balance that allows premature token readiness and potential price manipulation. This directly undermines the pool's economic security model.
paths: 1. Identify an initialized pool with an unready token (record.ready == false)
2. Option A (DoS): Send tokens directly to the pool address to inflate raw ERC20 balances
3. Call updateMinimumBalance(pool, token) - _estimatePoolValue reads inflated raw balances
4. minimumBalance = inflated_poolValue * token_price / 100 - set to an unreachable high value
5. Token can never reach minimum balance, permanently stuck in 'not ready' state
6. Option B (premature readiness): Withdraw from pool to deflate balances, call updateMinimumBalance to set near-zero minimum balance, then re-deposit
7. Token becomes ready immediately, enabling trading before the pool is properly balanced

title: _estimatePoolValue reads raw ERC20 balances instead of internal pool accounting, enabling donation-based pool value manipulation
claim: The current on-chain implementation changed _estimatePoolValue from using pool.extrapolatePoolValueFromToken() (which uses the pool's internal weight-adjusted accounting) to iterating over all current tokens and reading IERC20(token).balanceOf(pool) directly. This means any tokens sent directly to the pool address via ERC20 transfer (without calling joinPool) are counted in the pool value estimate. In Balancer-style pools, raw balances can exceed internal accounting due to direct transfers (the pool even has a gulp() function specifically to sync these). The function is used by reindexPool, updateMinimumBalance, and indirectly affects all rebalancing logic.
impact: An attacker can inflate the estimated pool value by donating tokens directly. When used by updateMinimumBalance (which lacks onlyOwner), this allows setting arbitrarily high minimum balances. When used by reindexPool (also permissionless), it causes incorrect minimum balances for newly indexed tokens, potentially making pool reindexing economically infeasible. The old implementation using extrapolatePoolValueFromToken was resistant to this attack vector.
paths: 1. Attacker identifies a pool with current tokens
2. Attacker transfers tokens directly to the pool address (NOT via joinPool) - these are not reflected in internal pool accounting
3. _estimatePoolValue() reads IERC20(token).balanceOf(pool) which now includes donated tokens
4. Estimated pool value is inflated beyond the true economic value of the pool
5. This inflated value feeds into updateMinimumBalance() and reindexPool() calculations
6. In reindexPool: minimumBalances for new tokens = inflated_totalValue * price / 100, requiring excessive token amounts
7. In updateMinimumBalance: minimumBalance set based on inflated value, potentially unreachable

title: reindexPool and reweighPool lack access control allowing anyone to trigger pool rebalancing
claim: Both reindexPool() and reweighPool() are declared as external with NO onlyOwner modifier. They have only internal timing checks (1-week delay and the 1-reindex-per-4-reweighs pattern). While this appears intentional for permissionless rebalancing, it means any actor can trigger pool rebalancing at will (subject to timing constraints). Combined with the fact that _estimatePoolValue reads raw balances, an attacker can manipulate the pool state before calling these functions.
impact: An attacker can front-run legitimate rebalancing with manipulated pool state (via token donations/withdrawals). They can also force reweighPool calls to consume the reweighIndex slots, preventing reindexPool from being called on schedule. Since reindexing changes the pool's entire token composition while reweighing only adjusts weights, disrupting this cycle can cause the pool to hold stale token compositions or incorrect weights.
paths: 1. Attacker monitors reweighIndex and lastReweigh for a target pool
2. When POOL_REWEIGH_DELAY has elapsed and reweighIndex % 4 == 3, reindexPool is callable
3. Attacker front-runs with a donation to the pool and calls reindexPool
4. The reindex uses inflated totalValue -> higher minimumBalances for new tokens
5. Alternatively, attacker calls reweighPool three consecutive times (each after 1-week delay) to force reweighIndex to advance, delaying the next reindex by 3 more weeks
6. The pool's token composition becomes stale, creating arbitrage opportunities

title: Unsafe subtraction without SafeMath causes underflow in _lastCategoryUpdate decrement
claim: The addToken(), addTokens(), and removeToken() functions use unchecked subtraction: _lastCategoryUpdate[categoryID] -= MAX_SORT_DELAY. The MarketCapSortedTokenCategories contract does not import SafeMath, and the Solidity 0.6.x compiler does not have built-in overflow protection. If _lastCategoryUpdate[categoryID] is less than MAX_SORT_DELAY (1 day), the subtraction underflows to a value near 2^256. This causes getTopCategoryTokens() to always revert with 'ERR_CATEGORY_NOT_READY' since now - huge_number overflows to a value > MAX_SORT_DELAY.
impact: While these functions are onlyOwner, if the owner adds a token to a newly created category before it has been sorted (or after a very recent sort), the category becomes permanently unusable for queries until orderCategoryTokensByMarketCap is called to reset _lastCategoryUpdate. This could cause temporary denial of service for pool operations that depend on category token ordering.
paths: 1. Owner creates a new category (categoryID = ++categoryIndex)
2. Owner immediately calls addToken(categoryID, token) before any sort
3. _lastCategoryUpdate[categoryID] is 0, subtracting MAX_SORT_DELAY underflows to ~2^256 - 86400
4. Any call to getTopCategoryTokens(categoryID, n) reverts because now - huge_number > MAX_SORT_DELAY
5. Owner must call orderCategoryTokensByMarketCap(categoryID) to reset the timestamp
6. Pool operations depending on this category (prepareIndexPool, reindexPool) will fail until sorted

title: orderCategoryTokensByMarketCap has no access control allowing anyone to re-sort token categories
claim: The orderCategoryTokensByMarketCap() function is external with only validCategory modifier (no onlyOwner). Anyone can trigger a re-sort of any category, which updates _lastCategoryUpdate[categoryID] = now and rearranges the _categoryTokens array by market cap. This directly affects which tokens are selected as the 'top' tokens for pool reindexing via getTopCategoryTokens().
impact: While likely intended for permissionless operation, an attacker can call this function at strategic times (e.g., when TWAP oracles show favorable prices for certain tokens) to manipulate which tokens appear as top tokens in a category. Combined with the ability to call reindexPool, an attacker could influence which tokens get included in an index pool. The attacker could also simply DoS by calling this function continuously to keep categories permanently 'ready' but with rapidly changing ordering.
paths: 1. Attacker monitors oracle TWAP prices for tokens in a category
2. When prices shift favorably, attacker calls orderCategoryTokensByMarketCap(categoryID)
3. The category is re-sorted based on current market caps, potentially changing token order
4. If reindexPool is then called, it uses getTopCategoryTokens which returns the newly ordered top N tokens
5. The pool gets reindexed with tokens favorable to the attacker's position
